beginner.question: What is CSS nesting?
beginner.answer: CSS nesting allows you to write nested selectors inside parent style rules, mirroring the HTML structure for more readable stylesheets.

beginner.question: What is the & nesting selector in CSS?
beginner.answer: The & nesting selector represents the parent selector, allowing you to reference the current element when writing nested rules.

beginner.question: How do you nest a pseudo-class like :hover using CSS nesting?
beginner.answer: Use &:hover inside the parent rule: .btn { &:hover { background: blue; } }

beginner.question: Can you nest media queries inside selectors with CSS nesting?
beginner.answer: Yes, @media rules can be nested directly inside style rules, scoping them to the parent selector context.

beginner.question: What is the syntax for nesting a child selector?
beginner.answer: Direct nesting without &: .card { .title { font-size: 1.5rem; } } nests .title inside .card.

beginner.question: How does CSS nesting differ from SCSS nesting?
beginner.answer: CSS nesting is the native browser feature with similar syntax; SCSS requires compilation while CSS nesting works directly in browsers.

beginner.question: Can you nest multiple levels in CSS nesting?
beginner.answer: Yes, you can nest selectors several levels deep, but deep nesting is discouraged for maintainability.

beginner.question: How do you use the & selector with a class or ID?
beginner.answer: Use &.active, &.highlight to append classes to the parent: .btn { &.active { background: green; } }

beginner.question: Does CSS nesting work with all selectors?
beginner.answer: Yes, nesting works with class, ID, element, attribute, pseudo-class, and pseudo-element selectors.

beginner.question: What browsers support native CSS nesting?
beginner.answer: All modern browsers support CSS nesting as of 2024-2025, including Chrome 120+, Firefox 117+, Safari 17.2+.

intermediate.question: How do you select a parent element from a nested child using CSS nesting?
intermediate.answer: You cannot select a parent from a child; the & selector only refers to the current nesting parent, not ancestors.

intermediate.question: How do you use the & selector with a combinator like > or +?
intermediate.answer: Use & > .child for direct children, & + .sibling for adjacent siblings, or & ~ .general for general siblings.

intermediate.question: How do you nest at-rules like @supports or @layer inside selectors?
intermediate.answer: Nest them directly without &: .card { @supports (display: grid) { display: grid; } }

intermediate.question: What happens when you omit the & in nested selectors?
intermediate.answer: Omitting & adds a space before the nested selector, creating a descendant selector: .card { .title {} } compiles to .card .title {}

intermediate.question: How do you create compound selectors with CSS nesting?
intermediate.answer: Use & without spaces: &#foo creates #foo, &.active creates .active.active, &:is(.special) creates compound conditions.

intermediate.question: Can CSS nesting be used inside @keyframes?
intermediate.answer: No, @keyframes cannot contain nested rules; nesting inside keyframes is not supported.

intermediate.question: How does CSS nesting affect specificity?
intermediate.answer: Nesting does not change specificity calculations; each nested rule's specificity is computed from the fully expanded selector.

intermediate.question: How do you nest naming conventions like BEM with CSS nesting?
intermediate.answer: Use &__title for BEM elements and &--modifier for BEM modifiers: .block { &__title {} &--active {} }

intermediate.question: How do you use & with multiple selectors for grouping?
intermediate.answer: Use &.class1, &.class2 inside the parent to group multiple variations: .btn { &.primary {} &.secondary {} }

intermediate.question: How do you nest deep selector patterns without excessive specificity?
intermediate.answer: Limit nesting depth to 2-3 levels and use &:is() or &:where() to manage specificity when nesting deeply.

advanced.question: How does CSS nesting interact with the :is() and :where() pseudo-classes?
advanced.answer: Nesting uses :is() internally for complex selectors; &:hover, &:focus becomes :is(.parent):hover:focus, maintaining correct specificity.

advanced.question: How do you nest media queries with different selector contexts?
advanced.answer: .responsive { @media (width > 768px) { &.wide { ... } } } targets .responsive.wide only when the media query matches.

advanced.question: What happens when you nest selectors inside @media that are already inside another selector?
advanced.answer: This creates deeply scoped media queries which remain valid; each level of nesting adds specificity to the final compiled selector.

advanced.question: How does CSS nesting handle the @scope at-rule?
advanced.answer: @scope can be nested inside selectors, allowing scoped styles within a nesting context without creating new cascade layers.

advanced.question: Can you use the & selector inside calc() or custom properties?
advanced.answer: No, & only works in selector contexts, not within property values. Use custom properties with different values instead.

advanced.question: How do you debug CSS nesting in browser DevTools?
advanced.answer: DevTools shows the computed/expanded selector in the Styles panel; the Sources panel shows the original nested source.

advanced.question: How do you combine CSS nesting with container queries?
How does CSS nesting handle the case where & is used with a pseudo-element?
advanced.answer: Use &::before and &::after directly: .card { &::before { content: ''; } } for pseudo-elements within the nesting context.

advanced.question: How do you use CSS nesting with @starting-style for entry animations?
advanced.answer: Nest @starting-style: .dialog { @starting-style { opacity: 0; } opacity: 1; } for smooth entry animations triggered on element addition.

advanced.question: How does CSS nesting interact with Shadow DOM style scoping?
advanced.answer: Inside Shadow DOM, nesting works normally; the & selector scopes to the shadow host, maintaining the shadow boundary encapsulation.
